data.title: TTM Technologies: AI And Defense Growth
data.description:
TTM Technologies (TTMI) is becoming a major AI and defense industry infrastructure player. I remember when I first spotted the company it was dependent on cheap consumer electronics and market fluctuations. Now the situation is different, 80% of the revenue comes from quite reliable and quality sectors - aviation, aerospace, defense and data centers. This change in portfolio is changing the game rules too: instead of short-term trends, company’s future now is dictated by decade-long defense programs and never-ending technology giant demand for computing power. I would even call it a classical transition story, where the company is becoming a strategic national security and technology advancement partner. Why is it important? Because TTMI is now in the market, where demand is almost guaranteed and competition faces high barriers to entry due to technology and regulation.

I believe that now is a good moment to have this stock in a watch list due to three reasons. First, AI infrastructure is experiencing bottleneck. New generation AI processors and servers need complex GPUs, multilayer Ultra-HDI substrates, which can manage intense heat and maintain data flow speed. TTMI data center segment is already demonstrating exceptional 57% annual growth, because big technology players do not have any other solution but just to use this highest level engineering.

Secondly, I believe that defense industry is in a supercycle. As geopolitical tensions are increasing each day, countries massively upgrade or renew their equipment. My country in particular is increasing defense budget. TTMI is one of the suppliers for critical systems like Patriot missiles or new generation radars. Backlog of $1.61 billion is the biggest in this companies history, and new orders are coming in 50% faster, than factories produce. This creates quite good understanding of what we should expect from revenue in the coming years.

Third, company reached operational ramp-up. Strategic investments into new factories in U.S. and Malaysia finally start showing returns. These modern facotries allows TTMI to manufacture more complex products with higher profits margin, while effectivelly managing manufacturing costs. As I said, I believe this is a good moment to watch the stock, as after market stops bashing AI stocks, it might run up.

AI Infrastructure Catalyst

I keep saying it like a buzzword, AI infrastructure. But when we talk about AI many people always start thinking about NVIDIA chips, while forgetting that the data is physically flowing in systems. Large language models like ChatGPT work like thousands of sports cars that are trying to speed through the highway. In reality that would lead to bottleneck unless someone decided to make 60 lanes highway. This is exactly what TTMI is creating. Their 40-60 layer circuit boards are those digital highways, without which those chips would simply overheat and "suffocate" due to data overload.

This technology is called Ultra-HDI. The engineering of this technology is extreme. The material is used to manage high server temperature and maintain signal integrity. At this moment, this is the main bottleneck in AI chain - chip demand is huge, but the world needs manufacturers that produce this complex infrastructure. I believe this is exactly why TTMI data center segment reported 57% annual growth in 2025.

I believe that big technology players like Alphabet and Microsoft will not spare money for companies like TTMI. It is expected that Alphabet will spend from $175 to $185 billion in 2026. I believe it is now widely known that we had a correction due to the market not knowing how to react to such extreme numbers. TTMI here is a direct beneficiary, because of their ability to manufacture substrates like PCBs (SLP) and Ultra-HDI circuit substrates for GPUs, which allows them to become main supplier for servers that use LLM models. As I said in many articles, I do not believe that AI is just a short-term trend, but fundamental digital infrastructure revolution.

Defense Sector

One of the most interesting projects for me is - LTMDS radars. These are new generation 360 view systems, created to detect and destroy high-complexity threats. TTMI is critical here, because their components allow radars to process data in real time without delays. Quite similar situation is with SPY-6 sea radars, that are used by U.S. Navy: TTMI is supplying large-format RF and circuit assemblies and integrated RF systems, which allow ships to see further and more precisely. Even those legendary Patriot missiles are based on TTMI engineering - company is making RF components, that are responsible for guidance and targeting systems.

While I know, that these are just details and investors want to understand the backlog. In the end of 2025 TTMI reported record backlog of $1.61 billion. To understand these numbers, we should use book-to-bill ratio, which is 1.46. In simple words, this ratio means good news, as this means that orders are flowing 50% faster than the company is manufacturing. The best part is that those numbers are not just on paper, they are almost guaranteed to become revenue. The company is busy, the demand is real and we can see their cash flows until 2027. This sector is like safety cushion for investors, even if one market slowed down, defense supercycle and government orders would maintain growth.

Competitive Advantage

Eau Claire: In July 2025, TTMI made a strategic move and instead of building a factory from scratch, they acquired 750,000 square feet infrastructure. This allowed them to save around 18-24 months, which would have been used for construction and permits. This solution is critical, because it lets them offer "made in U.S." AI infrastructure components especially when in 2026-2027 we are expecting tightest supply conditions.

Syracuse: New division is dedicated to Ultra-HDI technology, which is going to be used in new generation missiles and UAVs. The most important part is that TTMI here has "trusted foundry" status, given by Department of Defense. This is a huge barrier of entry to competitors, as just building a factory is not enough. Certification processes may take years and that just shows TTMI’s exceptional position and quite close relationship to clients like Raytheon.

Penang, Malaysia: I call it geopolitical shield. This factory is part of the strategy. It works as a safety in case west clients (especially hyperscalers), which due to safety policies or tariff risks are trying to diversify their supply chain away from China. Penang allows TTMI to maintain its commercial orders, that would be just taken by competitors, also allowing clients to diversify their supply.

Risks

The main risk that I see is operational one. The company at the same time is trying to ramp-up three huge factories, which is quite a load on management. Operational ramp-up process can experience labor shortage, manufacturing yield problems or supply chain problems. If any of these problems occur, I believe that market would lose trust in company’s ability to manage such growth.

Another risk is client concentration. Even though backlog is very good, five biggest clients in 2024 generated 44% of the revenue. In the defense sector TTMI is heavily dependent on giants like Raytheon or Lockheed Martin. Any change in governmental programs or decision to change supplying strategy could have direct impact and a painful one on finances. All in all, it is interesting that TTMI is a "hostage" of its own success - their ability to expand manufacturing is going to be watched by the whole market.

Valuation

My DCF valuation shows that TTM Technologies is undervalued at current market price. I believe that the stock has upside potential of around 30-35%. I have used 106.7 million fully diluted shares (included options and RSU to capture realistic future capital structure). WACC was 8.86% based on 4.25% risk free rate, 1.0 beta and 5% risk premium, all of this is based on more stable business profile due to defense and AI infrastructrue demand.

Expected revenue growth begins from 17.5% and naturally decreases to long-term levels, EBITDA margin expands to 20% due to higher value product and manufacturing efectivness. I have decided to decrease CapEx when projects should end. Also used 18x EV/EBITDA terminal ratio which I believe reflect AI and defense segment permiums. Finally, my DCF showed quite attractive risk to reward ratio.

Summary

The company transformed from cyclical electronics supplier to strategic AI and defense infrastructure player. Around 80% of the revenue comes from aviation, defense and data center segments, where demand seems to be long-term and stable. Many believe that the company is overvalued, but investment here is not a value play, rather it is momentum and I believe that AI technology is just starting out and peak is still far away. I am rating the company a Strong Buy.

data.title: Blackstone Mortgage Trust: Stabilization Achieved, Risks Still Linger
data.description:
My thesis

In my view, Blackstone Mortgage Trust (BXMT) is starting 2026 with a stabilized, but structurally sensitive portfolio. In 2025, the company earned $110 million in net income, and this marks a comeback to profitability from the previous downturn. Overall investment portfolio grew to $20 billion, though its composition changed significantly. The office sector's part decreased to 29%, when the previous year it consisted of 35%. US office exposure is still reaching 22% of the whole portfolio, so this portion remains the main risk. In 2025, the company closed new deals worth $6-7 billion, focusing on multifamily and industrial real estate. These segments currently make up around 50% of the whole loan portfolio. Even though 99% of the portfolio is currently performing, $970 million worth of problematic assets still require additional management. The P/E ratio is over 30, and this is more than double the amount for the sector's average of 12.4x in real estate mortgage investment trusts. The company's revenues will be directly dependent on the dynamics of variable interest rates, because a big part of loans is linked to floating interest rates. The market values this company with a premium, hoping for a successful realization of problematic assets over the next 12 months.

Business overview and product rotation

Blackstone Mortgage Trust is a real estate financing company and manages over a $20 billion loan portfolio. In 2025, they earned $110 million in net income, and EPS reached $0.64. Although the distributable earnings before write-offs were $1.86 per share, the factual results after realized credit losses dropped to -$1.43. The company's liquidity reserves at the end of 2025 reached $1.0 billion, and the debt-to-equity ratio was 3.9x. This matches the sector's averages, though capital structure remains sensitive to interest rate fluctuations. 99% of the loan portfolio is performing, and this suggests stability after a decline in 2024. Book value per share reaches $20.75, which is around 12% higher than the market's price at the start of 2026.

The company is executing an aggressive portfolio rotation, decreasing risk in the office sector. From the end of 2021, positions in the office sector decreased 50%. Currently, US office loans make up only 20% of the net portfolio value. In 2025, BXMT invested $6.8 billion, of which 85% was directed to financing apartment residential buildings and industrial facilities. These 2 segments now consist of 50% of the whole loan portfolio. The company also diversified their activity, purchasing bank loan portfolios with a discount and expanding its net lease strategy, which accounts for 5% of total assets. The European market takes up 40% of the whole loan portion, which decreases dependency on US real estate cycles. In 2025 Q4, all new loans worth $1.4 billion were given only for the industrial and apartment sectors. This shows the final withdrawal from the speculative office financing model.

Comparison

Blackstone Mortgage Trust's forecasted forward P/E is reaching 11.9x, and this suggests that the market believes in its recovery after the 2024 dividend decrease. Compared to its main competitor, Starwood Property Trust (STWD), which has a forward P/E of 9.9x, BXMT shares in the market are at a 20% premium. This difference shows investors' hopeful expectations for Blackstone's platform scale and the ability to quickly restructure the problematic office sector, though STWD has historically always been considered a more diversified player. BXMT's price-to-book ratio currently is 0.94x, which is close to 3 year high and suggests that the shares are traded at a small discount to the nominal value of the asset. While STWD's P/B ratio is at 0.89, which means that the market values its assets with more caution, even though its forward P/E is lower. This disproportion suggests that BXMT's capital structure and quality of assets under management after write-offs are valued as a more transparent or less risky company in the long term.

Analyzing other sectors' competitors, for example, Apollo Commercial Real Estate Finance (ARI), we can see an even bigger gap. ARI's P/B ratio is currently reaching 0.79, and this indicates a deep market's lack of confidence in their loan portfolio or specific risks related to their portfolio sale deal to the Athene group. ARI's P/E ratio is reaching 13.3, though this number is less informative because of their current strategic transformation and asset realization processes. BXMT, from this point of view, seems like a better commercial real estate loan model.

Valuation

Blackstone Mortgage Trust's valuation shows the market's belief in its portfolio stabilization, though there is no big safety reserve left. Shares are traded at 0.94 P/B, which is a huge improvement compared to 2024's discounts, though this also means that the market has already included a moderate slowdown scenario. The main attraction for investors is the 9.6% dividend yield; however, its sustainability is fragile: the $0.47 quarterly dividend is covered by only $0.51 of distributable earnings before write-offs, and GAAP profit is still technically negative because of asset write-offs. A positive signal is that CECL reserves in a year shrank 60%, which suggests that a big part of problematic office loans are cleared from the balance sheet. Still paying almost full price for a company's book value, which has limited growth, investors take a risk that interest rate reduction in 2026 could further pressure net interest income.

Share buybacks and dividends

Blackstone Mortgage Trust in 2025 Q4 maintained a $0.47 dividend per share, which makes a yearly payout of $1.88 and generates around 9.6% yield. Even though the company reported around $110 million GAAP for 2025, distributable EPS after write-offs was negative -$1.43, though the adjusted figure before write-offs reached $0.51 per quarter and fully covered the dividend payment. Management aggressively reacted to the decrease in share price below its book value and, in 2025, finished a $138.6 million share buyback program. These buybacks increased book value by around $0.30 per share, and management approved a new $150 million buyback program for 2026. This shows a shift in capital allocation: the company is choosing to buy back their shares with a discount instead of investing in new loans when the market's conditions remain complicated. For investors, this signals that dividends for now are paid from operating income, though the long-term sustainability will depend on credit loss stabilization, which in Q4 still reached $434 million in the form of reserves and write-offs.

Risks

Blackstone Mortgage Trust's cleanup was costly; though 99% of the portfolio is now performing, it was only reached by realizing huge losses, which lowered the book value to $20.75 per share. In 2025 Q4, the company wrote off $434 million worth of bad loans, so the official distributable EPS dropped to a negative figure, though the adjusted figure was $0.51 and covered a $0.47 dividend. This reveals that dividend sustainability is still balancing on an edge and depends on management’s use of adjusted earnings that exclude realized write-offs. The biggest structural risk remains the office sector, which still contains 27% of the whole portfolio. Issued loans prior to 2022 are the most affected by the change in property value. Although liquidity is reaching $1.0 billion and the debt-to-equity ratio is at 3.9x, any further decline in property value in the office segment will directly lower already shrunken capital, leaving no room for error.

Thesis summary

In my opinion, Blackstone Mortgage Trust is starting 2026 with a stable, though still structurally sensitive, portfolio. After significant write-offs, the company managed to return the portfolio's effectiveness and lower the office sector's concentration, though this segment remains the main risk, especially when talking about the older loans, which were given out before the real estate value adjustment. Dividends are currently paid with adjusted distributable income, though GAAP results are still weighed down by realized losses, so dividend sustainability still depends on further credit stabilization

Valuation's perspective: shares are traded close to their book value, and this suggests that the market has already priced in the portfolio's stabilization and is expecting successful problematic loan realization. Even though an almost 10% dividend yield remains attractive to income investors, further returns will depend on interest rate dynamics and potential additional credit losses. So the current risk and return ratio seems balanced: the company is showing signs of stabilization, though its limited growth potential and remaining structural risk justify a more cautious, monitoring stance.

data.title: Global Economic Outlook: February 2026
data.description:
How is geopolitical uncertainty influencing the global economic outlook?

Geopolitical events and related uncertainties have contributed to choppy commodity and financial markets, but S&P Global’s Purchasing Managers Indexes (PMIs®) point to relatively resilient economic conditions.

At around $68 per barrel at the time of writing, the price of Dated Brent crude oil is below its peak year-to-date but still more than 10% above its end-2025 level, amid ongoing concerns about potential disruption to Middle Eastern supply. Prices of precious metals are below their January record highs but remain very elevated.

US equity markets have come under pressure, with the S&P 500 recording back-to-back weekly losses in the first half of February. The tech-heavy NASDAQ composite index has continued its recent underperformance, losing ground for five straight weeks. The US Treasury market has benefited from a related ‘flight to safety,’ with 10-year yields falling to their lowest level since November. Narrowing yield differentials are forecast to continue to lean down on the US dollar.

What does our Purchasing Managers Index data tell us about the economy?

January’s PMI® data signaled a pick-up in global growth momentum, although output expectations remained lackluster.

The global composite output index, based on manufacturing and services activity across more than 40 economies, rose by half a percentage point in January. The level of the composite index has historically been indicative of sub-3% global real GDP growth, broadly aligning with our forecast. January’s improvement reflected a marked pick-up in the manufacturing output index, which matched its highest level since June 2024. Manufacturing new orders expanded at the fastest rate for almost a year.

Global services activity also picked up, although the implied rate of expansion remained below its average during the fourth quarter of 2025. The signals from January’s output expectations data were less encouraging, with geopolitical uncertainties — particularly related to trade — again flagged by survey participants as a prime concern.

What are the key changes in our global economic outlook this month?

Key changes in our February forecast update include higher projections for real GDP growth in the US and India this year, pushing up our global growth forecast.

Our US annual real GDP growth forecast for 2026 was raised to half a percentage point above 2025’s estimated rate of growth. This primarily reflects “carry over” effects due to a substantial markup to expected US growth in the final quarter of 2025, captured in our tracking estimates. The growth profile through 2026 is also a bit stronger, consistent with somewhat more favorable financial conditions and a larger inventory rebuild than previously forecast.

The more positive assessment of India's annual real GDP growth forecasts for the current and next fiscal year reflects news from the budget and recent trade agreements. This year’s eurozone growth forecast has also been edged up, helped by a more positive assessment for Germany.

Our annual global real GDP growth forecast for 2026 has risen to 2.9%, matching 2025’s estimate and above the market consensus expectation — where it has been since mid-2025 amid ongoing economic resilience.

What are our expectations for consumer price inflation?

We forecast somewhat lower annual consumer price inflation rates in 2026 in most major economies, but recent developments have flagged some upside risks. The projected moderations in inflation are driven by two key factors: an expected decline in crude oil prices and an easing of underlying price pressures.

The recent news on both has been mixed. For the former, concerns over potential disruption to Middle East supply have kept oil prices elevated. For the latter, although core inflation data late in 2025 showed some encouraging signs of moderation, January’s PMI® figures pointed to some emerging cost pressures.

The assumptions for oil prices which fed into our February forecast update were lifted in the very short term, but supply-driven declines are still projected later in 2026. The base case is an average Dated Brent price of $63/b in the first quarter, with a trough of around $56/b in mid-year, followed by a gradual rise thereafter as supply and demand move more into balance.

Inflation rates in India and mainland China are forecast to buck the trend and rise in 2026. In the former, unfavorable base effects related to weak food price inflation in 2025 are the key factor. In the latter, anti-involution initiatives and policy stimulus aimed at supporting consumer demand are expected to diminish disinflationary pressures, although inflation is forecast to remain low.

What else are we watching this month?

Fundamental drivers continue to favor further US dollar depreciation. January’s slide was temporarily reversed by the US administration’s nomination of Kevin Warsh to chair the Federal Reserve, but the rebound faded quite quickly.

Following 2025’s near-4% drop, our model-based projections for the broad nominal effective US dollar index incorporate somewhat smaller annual declines out to 2028, consistent with narrowing yield differentials and persistent external imbalances. Downside risks include capital repatriation and adverse US economic news, including on the labor market.

January’s US employment report was positive at face value, but the data were overshadowed by large downward revisions to prior payroll gains in the annual benchmarking. Employment gains have also been concentrated in healthcare-related areas.

Futures markets continue to discount a pause in Fed rate cuts until mid-2026 and only gradual easing through this year. At the time of writing, an 80% chance of a cut of 25 basis points was discounted by June’s policy meeting, followed by a return to the estimated neutral range of 3.00-3.25% in the fall. These expectations broadly align with our forecasts.

This article was published by S&P Global Market Intelligence and not by S&P Global Ratings, which is a separately managed division of S&P Global.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Why MJ Is No Longer A Sell
data.description:
19 weeks ago, I initiated coverage of the Amplify Alternative Harvest ETF (MJ) with a Sell, and it has declined by almost 33%. While I remain concerned about this cannabis ETF, I am upgrading it to Hold.

A Look at MJ

When I reviewed this ETF in October, the ETF had 5.27 million shares outstanding, and there are now 4.97 million. At $25.58, the ETF has a market cap of just $127 million. I pointed to its structure of holding another fund, Amplify Seymour Cannabis ETF (CNBS), and some cannabis stocks. There were nine investments at that time, and there are currently nine (as of 2/18):

The amount of CNBS has increased from 47.5%, while each of the names that were in the ETF then are still there now. The amount of Tilray Brands, Inc. (TLRY) has declined from 22.7%, but this is due to a much lower price currently than then.

CNBS currently has very large exposure to a few names, with Trulieve Cannabis Corp. (TCNNF), Green Thumb Industries Inc. (GTBIF), and Curaleaf Holdings, Inc. (CURLF) all above 10%.

The ETF trades near its NAV, and the volume was very high in December due to excitement over potential cannabis rescheduling. The stock ran up from a low of $23.66 in November to as high as $46.75 in December, but it has come almost all the way back to its six-month low:

The 52-week low (and all-time low) was set in March and then tested in April, leaving a potential double-bottom near $16. I see support at $25 and then at $20, and I see resistance at $30.

Since 10/10, the New Cannabis Ventures Global Cannabis Stock Index has plunged 22.9%, while MJ has dropped by more, falling 32.8%. Its largest holding, CNBS, has declined by 55.2%, and TLRY, which was 22.7% of the ETF, has declined by 55.9%.

How MJ Compares to Other Cannabis ETFs

The cannabis space does not have very many ETFs, as some have closed. The biggest ETF, AdvisorShares Pure US Cannabis ETF (MSOS), is similar to CNBS. It is even more heavily exposed to Curaleaf, GTI, and Trulieve, with a total exposure currently of 68.2% to these three. The ETF is down 19.1% year-to-date, which is slightly worse than CNBS, which has dropped 18.7%. AdvisorShares Pure Cannabis ETF (YOLO) is similar to MJ, and it is down 12.1% year-to-date, while MJ has dropped 14.1%. Here is the action on all four of these since 6/30/25:

Cannabis stocks have bounced from the lows set in 2025, but they are down substantially from where they were trading five years ago.

What It Takes for MJ to Do Better

I think the biggest driver for MJ would be the elimination of 280E taxation, which rescheduling, if it takes place, would accomplish. More than half of the portfolio is in CNBS, which has substantial exposure to MSOs. The rest of MJ is invested in Canadian LPs, and it is possible that the country could eliminate the cultivation tax, which would help these companies tremendously. The Canadian LPs are also exposed to international markets, and expansion in other countries could help these companies.

How MJ Could Suffer a Larger Decline

If 280E sticks around, which is very possible, it will likely weigh on the MSOs, and MJ has a lot of exposure to them through its investment in CNBS, which I have rated as a Hold. There could be more financial challenges for MSOs if 280E remains in place. While MJ and CNBS haven't seen the types of inflows that AdvisorShares funds have experienced, if MSOS gets redemptions, it could weigh on the stocks it and CNBS own. MJ is also very exposed to Canadian LPs, and the Canadian market and other international markets may face challenges that would weigh on MJ.

Conclusion

MJ has been volatile, and it could climb again or plunge. I think the underlying stocks are very cheap, but the American cannabis operators face big risks. My model portfolio at 420 Investor has a lot of cash right now (19.8%), and I am very underweight in the MSOs. I am somewhat overweight in the Canadian LPs, but the three Canadian LPs that have the two largest positions in the model portfolio are not that big in MJ. I do own a small amount of its largest Canadian LP holding. The model portfolio holds three ancillary cannabis stocks that total 32.7% of the portfolio, but MJ does not hold any of those sub-sectors.

I understand how many investors prefer ETFs to cannabis stocks, but I don't believe that there are any good ETFs for the cannabis sector. My negativity towards MJ in October was based on several reasons. Since then, MSOs have plunged, while Canadian LPs have declined to a lesser degree. The price of MJ is much lower, and the gap that was left behind in December has been filled. MJ could keep dropping, or it could rally. I am upgrading it from Sell to Hold.

data.title: Commodities: Trump Sets A Deadline For Iranian Deal
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Head of Commodities Strategy, and Ewa Manthey, Commodities Strategist

Energy - US oil inventory declines add to bullish sentiment

“We’re either going to get a deal, or it’s going to be unfortunate for them,” were the words of US President Donald Trump as he put pressure on Iran to come to a nuclear deal. The escalation we have seen in recent days with the build-up of US military assets in the region makes it increasingly difficult to find a path to de-escalation. However, President Trump has given Iran 10-15 days to agree to a deal. Failure to do so would likely lead to US military action against Iran. The key question is then how prolonged any action would be and what the ultimate aim of the US is. A short and targeted campaign with limited retaliation from Iran (similar to what was seen in June 2025) is starting to look like the best-case scenario. This would likely only lead to a short-term spike in oil prices.

However, prolonged action from the US and less measured retaliation from Iran would be more concerning for oil markets, increasingly posing a risk to Iranian and broader Persian Gulf energy infrastructure. In addition, if Iran feels that the aim of any action is more about regime change, we could see a much more aggressive response. The heightened uncertainty that the market faces over the next two weeks suggests that oil prices are likely to continue to price in a large risk premium.

US oil inventory data from the Energy Information Administration (EIA) yesterday would have only provided further support to the oil market, with a bullish release. US crude oil inventories fell by a little more than 9m barrels over the last week, with exports jumping 851k b/d week-on-week, while imports fell 281k b/d WoW. Meanwhile, refined products also saw inventory declines, with gasoline and distillate stocks falling 3.21m barrels and 4.57m barrels, respectively. The fall in refined product stocks comes despite strong refinery utilisation rates over the week. An increase in implied demand offset the stronger refinery activity.

European natural gas prices rallied further yesterday amid the growing tension in the Middle East. TTF settled 6.5% higher on the day at EUR33.52/MWh. Escalation in the Middle East would leave a sizeable amount of global LNG trade at risk, and at a time when the EU holds lower than usual gas inventories. EU gas storage is less than 32% full at the moment, well below the five-year average of 49% full.

For US natural gas, inventories fell by 144bcf over the week, slightly less than the 149bcf the market was expecting. This leaves total US natural gas storage at 2.07tcf, which is 2.8% below year-ago levels and also 5.6% below the five-year average.

Metals - Gold edges higher towards $5,000/oz

Gold is trading around the $5,000/oz mark, recovering from recent volatility as investors reassess geopolitical risks and the broader macro backdrop.

Markets remain sensitive to US-Iran talks, with lingering uncertainty helping to keep gold well-supported near record levels.

The underlying outlook for gold remains constructive. Geopolitical risks, expectations of lower interest rates later this year, and continued investor and central bank demand are underpinning prices. While volatility is likely to stay elevated around geopolitical headlines, risks remain skewed to the upside, even if gains from here are likely to be more measured than the sharp rally seen previously.

Agriculture - USDA 2026 planting estimates

In its first planting outlook for 2026, the USDA estimates soybean plantings will rise, while corn and wheat acreage will decline. The agency projects 2026 soybean acreage at 85m acres, higher than the 81.2m acres planted in 2025 and largely in line with the average market expectations of 85m acres. In contrast, the USDA projects 2026 corn plantings at 94m acres, below the 98.8m acres planted in 2025 and the average market expectation of 95m acres. Wheat planting estimates are expected to fall slightly to 45m acres, lower than the 45.3m acres planted last year but higher than the average market expectation of 44.7m acres.

Turning to the ending stocks, the agency projects US soybean ending stocks could rise 1.4% year-on-year to 355m bushels for the 2026/27 season, below the average market expectations of 361m bushels. Similarly, the USDA projects 2026/27 wheat ending stocks to reach 933m bushels, slightly higher than the 931m bushels at the end of the 2025/26 season. The market was expecting a number closer to 887m bushels. Corn ending stock estimates are expected to fall from 2,127m bushels in 2025/26 to 1,837m bushels in 2026/27, reflecting the impact of lower acreage.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: UAE: Motoring Along Nicely With Some Fine Tailwinds To Support It
data.description:
Background

The iShares MSCI UAE ETF (UAE), which has been in existence for nearly 12 years, is a passively managed ETF (it tracks the MSCI All UAE Capped Index) that covers 55 stocks from the federal semi-constitutional monarchy- UAE. We’ve written about UAE thrice over the last 4 years, but it was only in September 2025 that we decided to turn bullish on this product.

Our optimism was centered around the strength of the non-oil segment (which reflects well on UAE’s diversification efforts), the favorable prospects of its two largest holdings- Emaar Properties [EMAAR] and First Abu Dhabi Bank [FAB], which together accounted for over one-fourth of the portfolio, a comforting yield, and cheap valuations.

Since the upgrade, UAE has done pretty well to witness an expansion of 17%, which is in line with its emerging market peers, while also managing to outperform global markets by over 2.2x.

So far so good, but do we feel confident enough to maintain our bullish stance? Yes, and here’s why we are not reneging on our optimism.

Ongoing Economic Buoyancy, And Healthy Conditions for the Financial Sector

The first thing to be said is that if you want to garner exposure to the fastest growing region in the GCC [Gulf Cooperation Council], UAE is the place to be. Real GDP growth for UAE has been the best in this region for 3 years on the trot (last year even Saudi Arabia kept pace), and even in FY26, UAE is expected to lead the rest of the pack with growth of close to 5%, well beyond the regional average of around 3%.

Investors should also consider that contrary to popular belief, UAE is not solely dependent on oil revenue; in fact the non-oil sector is expected to grow at a faster pace ( 5.5%) than the overall economy, and will account for 78% of the total economy (last year it already contributed 75% of GDP). This ongoing diversification is very useful as it increasingly insulates UAE from the wild swings of the oil market.

With the GDP foundation in good shape, you ideally want to stay exposed to cyclical sectors that could grow at +1x pace of GDP growth, and UAE iw well set to offer this. UAE covers stocks from 10 different sectors, but almost 40% of the portfolio consists of financial stocks alone.

These financials are in a good way as demand for credit remains very high; note that loan growth at 18% is at decade highs and coming in at over 3x the pace of GDP growth. While the strong base year of FY25 may result in the YoY pace slowing down in FY26, we can’t imagine loan appetite disappearing overnight, given that UAE corporates have some of the most elevated CAPEX bills (in the GCC region) that will need to be supported with credit.

Investors should also consider that UAE is also increasingly becoming the prime magnet for attracting high net worth individuals and millionaires (last year the country saw close to 10000 millionaires relocate, which was a record). This also positions these financial services stocks to profit from high fee-based servicing opportunities that could help ameliorate any pressures that could arise if the credit environment were to turn for the worse.

Compelling Income Profile and a Useful Diversifier

When we last covered UAE, we had pitched its yield of 3.25% as a supporting factor, as it was better than what ETFs in general were yielding. Now despite, the ETF witnessing a price appreciation, note that UAE’s dividend yield to 3.57%, has still improved even at these higher prices. This is backed by exceptional dividend growth were last year’s dividends grew at almost 8x the pace of the median ETF dividend growth (5.5%).

UAE’s strong dividend profile is founded on the dividend principles of its top two holdings- Emaar and FAB. Admittedly the former isn’t growing its dividends, but at the end of the day, it is faced with plenty of growth opportunities where cash needs to be reinvested (it is still paying a decent payout of 50% of its net profits of AED 8.8B) and yet still,the current dividends it pays is translating to impressive yields of+6%.

FAB’s recent payout is a tad lower than Emaar at 43%, but its dividend hike most recently of 7% YoY was better than what was seen in the previous year (6%).

Some investors with strong US-centric equity portfolios may also be concerned over the growing level of froth in the domestic market especially on the giant and large-cap front. If you’re looking to insulate and diversify these domestic-heavy portfolios, UAE may come across as a useful tool as it is not very sensitive to the movements of the S&P500. This is reiterated by an equity beta of only 0.24x over the last 3 years.

Still Offers Great Value Vs Global Markets, And Potential for Further Mean Reversion

One of the major incentives for pursuing UAE back in September, was that its valuations appeared to be very compelling vs global stocks. This mismatch we felt would drive further rotation into this counter, helping prop up the low relative strength ratio (vs global stocks) to its long-term mean of 0.205.

While we have seen the RS ratio see some upward mean reversion from the 0.139 levels, it is a still over 25% below its long-term mean, offering further upward scope of reversion.

What makes things even more alluring is that the valuation differential between UAE and the globe. Back in September, UAE was priced at forward earnings multiple of double-digits, now the forward earnings have expanded to such a extent, that despite UAE seeing price appreciation its valuations have dropped to less than 9x P/E. Basically, UAE’s valuation differential vs the globe which was at +40% levels back then, is now at +50% levels (VT ETF, the proxy of the globe, is now priced at less than 19x).

Technicals- A Breakout Supplemented By Strong Momentum

UAE’s technicals now are in a more resolute state. The last time we covered it, it was expressing some loss of momentum but still defending its 200DMA; now it is seeing strong momentum, with a series of higher-highs and higher-lows and also trading above its other two key shorter moving averages of 50 and 100, besides the 200DMA.

Supplementing this, we have the ADX (average directional index) indicator, which measures the strength of a trend, trading at 40 levels (strong trending conditions are usually flagged when the indicator crosses 25).

Meanwhile, switch over to the larger time frame- the monthly chart, and you’ll see that UAE’s price movements over the last decade have taken place within a rising wedge (two upward sloping converging lines). This month we’ve seen UAE break out of the upper line, after having spent a good 6 months consolidating just below the breakout zone.

Conclusion

On account of the favorable macro backdrop, the tilt towards financial stocks, a comforting income position, some useful diversification qualities, cheap valuations, and a robust technical picture we reiterate our buy on UAE.

data.title: Walmart Q4: Too Expensive To Support Forward Operating Income Growth Expectations
data.description:
Walmart Inc. (WMT) reported earnings for Q4 2026 and FY 2026 yesterday before the market opened, and the reaction from the market was underwhelming, with the stock down 1.4 percent over Thursday's trading. The longer-term decline that began last week is yet to fully play out, it seems. For its part, WMT did exceedingly well on all fronts, but I did find it odd that the CEO would address profitability over sales growth right out of the gate.

After digging deeper into the numbers, I found it to be a very astute move that, unfortunately, failed to rally investors. Operating profit growth seemed to be the central theme of the earnings call, but the problem is that its outlook of 7 percent (midpoint) in operating income growth in FY 2027 was just not enough to offset the market's worries about WMT's valuation metrics, which are unreasonably high. I hereby initiate my thesis for WMT with a Hold.

Q4 2026 Snapshot

Management was quick to highlight operating leverage across its segments - "All 3 segments grew profits faster than sales for the quarter". I find it odd that the CEO brought up a seemingly insignificant win as the first point of discussion, because a retailer like WMT typically faces razor-thin margins and very little evidence of operating leverage.

Over Q2 and Q3 2026 (two quarters preceding the current Q4 report; not included in the table above as Seeking Alpha is yet to update the information as of this writing), operating income margins have actually shown year-on-year declines on a GAAP basis.

This is why the CEO's statement about Q4 profitability growing faster than sales is important. It is the first sign that operational efficiencies are being reflected in reported ops margins. A 5.6 percent uplift in sales resulted in a 10.8 percent increase in operating income.

These efficiencies are primarily coming from automating two key cost centres - inventory and labor. The rest of the operating income growth is coming from avenues like advertising, membership fees, and third-party or 3P marketplace.

On the automation front, WMT is about halfway there:

In Walmart U.S., approximately 60% of stores receive some freight from automated distribution centers, and approximately 50% of the eCommerce fulfillment center volume is automated. This enables better visibility into what inventory we own and what inventory we can access, and also improves our labor productivity. With the proximity so close to customers, we're increasingly leveraging stores as digital fulfillment nodes to move inventory faster and more efficiently than ever before.

The supply chain for international markets is naturally more complex and capital-intensive, but even in the early stages, this is likely to ease margin pressure to a degree.

What is clear is that this operating income growth is only the beginning of the many benefits WMT can reap from AI and automation. It is equally clear that the price run-up since the second half of November 2025 was overdone.

The rest of the Q4 print was more or less in line with expectations (a double-beat but missed on GAAP EPS), but the market did not seem to be in a very rewarding mood. The bearish sentiment that set in last week continued after a brief pop, presumably due to the double-beat.

Valuation Too Rich for 7 Percent Operating Income Growth Outlook in FY 2027

Part of the reason I believe the market is behaving this way (notwithstanding the general decline one is seeing in tech and the sideways movement of the S&P 500 (SP500)) is that WMT investors came into Q4 earnings with great expectations that rapidly fizzled out.

After hitting a high of $134-$135 a few days before earnings, the cooling-off period only served to prolong an ongoing decline. One contributing factor could be the tepid guidance for FY 2027 operating income growth.

The worst of it is that I do not think it is over yet, but I cannot rate it a Sell because this is still a very fundamentally attractive company that has delivered market-beating returns for well over a decade.

This is a textbook case of a great company at a not-so-great price. The market tends to get ahead of itself, and by the time it realizes that the stock is trading at nearly 50 times forward adjusted earnings, with an enterprise value of 35 times forward EBIT, most profit-takers are already out the door, leaving new investors holding the bag.

I say, if you are indeed holding the bag, hold it for much longer. It will be several quarters before we see the full effect of operational efficiencies driving profitability, but we should simultaneously see the valuation gap contract. The problem is that it will take years for that to filter down to the bottom line, and Street analysts seem to agree. They are currently predicting double-digit EPS growth over the next three years, but at 35 times FY 2029 earnings, I think the stock is too expensive.

What Can WMT Investors Do?

For long-term investors, a Hold rating just means having the patience to weather the current price decline. For new investors, this is a 'do not buy now' signal because the correction may not be over yet.

Seen through that lens, one can immediately see why it was important for Mr. Furner to begin with the profitability theme at the earnings call. Profitability, per se, is not the problem; the real problem is the gap between profitability metrics and share price. I see WMT doing its best to fill that gap as quickly as possible, but as I hope this analysis has shown, it will likely be a long journey.

More aggressive investors may want to trim their holdings to protect from further downside or buy put protection. March 20 puts for a strike of $120 are currently about $180 per contract, so that is one option to consider. You could offset that by selling March 20 calls at a $130 strike for $185, and that is around the one-year price target seen on Yahoo, so you would not be giving up any upside potential.

Overall, my view is that this is a risky bet for new money, and if you are worried about a further correction, a protective put with an offsetting sold call would be a reasonable strategy.

data.title: Sixth Street Specialty Lending: Alpha From Underwriting
data.description:
Company Description

Sixth Street Specialty Lending (TSLX) seeks to generate current income primarily in U.S.-domiciled middle-market companies through direct originations of senior secured loans and, to a lesser extent, originations of mezzanine and unsecured loans and investments in corporate bonds and equity securities. TSLX defines middle market companies as those with EBITDA between $10 million and $250 million (portfolio-weighted EBITDA was $127 million). TSLX is the 8th largest BDC by market capitalization and among the largest in terms of total assets ($3.3B at fair value as of 12/31/25).

Investment Thesis and Recommendation

Let me begin with the fact that Sixth Street Specialty Lending is one of my favorite business development companies. I consider the BDC to be the public sleeve of this private credit shop. Sixth Street is not a “vanilla” BDC; they are focused on adding value through their ability to underwrite complex situations and companies.

  • TSLX invests primarily in first lien senior secured loans to non-investment-grade companies, helping mitigate the potential for losses due to default.
  • Portfolio quality remains solid, with non-accruals at a reasonable level and the majority of the portfolio falling into the two highest rating categories.
  • While the portfolio is well diversified by borrower, it must be noted that their total exposure to software is approximately 40 percent.
  • While portfolio yield has fallen due mainly to a decline in SOFR as well as some spread compression, it remains among the highest in the peer group due to their focus on complexity.
  • The dividend remains stable and should continue to do so as it is well covered and supported by spillover.
  • While TSLX trades at a premium to NAV of 113%, this is near its one year and three-year lows, reflecting the sector headwinds facing BDCs and private credit generally.
  • The recent formation of Structured Credit Partners, or SCP, a joint venture between BDCs managed by Sixth Street and the Carlyle Group, should be accretive to the company, as the manager expects a mid-teens ROIC.

The company is fundamentally a BUY at these levels, given their financial performance and their ability to create alpha throughout difficult or challenging periods. The sector headwinds, however, keep me from labeling it a BUY. I realize that this sounds like (or is) trying to “time the market,” but I am not trying to call the bottom; I am merely waiting for more color on the sector, which should be provided with the earnings we have coming up. It does concern me that the BDC has upwards of 40 percent software exposure (more on this later), which has been a flashpoint within the sector, but this concern is somewhat mitigated through their underwriting standards. I do not believe we have seen the end of NAV and NII erosion, as this is simply part of the cycle. It is in this environment that I believe TSLX will outperform peers with their approach. I believe, however, that investors have the ability to be “patient capital” until we get a better look under the hood of the sector.

Bottom Line: HOLD. As I have said for other BDCs, if you are considering entering the name, I would emphasize patience. If you have to scratch the itch, leg into the position and don’t take your full size immediately. Sometimes doing nothing is the hardest trade. Why not a SELL? Fundamentally, the company has been doing well, and much of this is part of the cycle (not the AI menacing the software sector; that is NOT cyclical). Why not a BUY? The sector is being pressured from all sides, giving investors the flexibility to be patient. Unless something significant/idiosyncratic occurs, TSLX will be one of the first BDCs I will flip to a BUY.

It is worth noting that having a differentiated approach like TSLX lends itself to pairing up with other more “vanilla” BDCs, as there is the diversification benefit (similar to adding venture focused BDCs like Hercules, Trinity, or Runway).

The Manager

Sixth Street is a leading credit manager, with experience in private credit, public credit, bank loans, restructurings, and secondary investments. Sixth Street manages approximately $125 billion across multi-strategy credit and opportunistic investing, with approximately 78 professionals dedicated to direct lending. TSLX does not currently offer an evergreen or interval fund, making TSLX their only publicly traded (or non-traded) access to their credit platform.

Portfolio Profile

The portfolio is intentionally weighted toward the top of the capital structure to provide downside protection but also has more equity than many of its peers, helping increase returns. Senior secured investments represent 89 percent of the total portfolio at fair value (there are unitranches—amount undisclosed—that have their own nuance). In the fourth quarter’s funding activity, 97 percent of the company’s investments were in first lien loans, a pickup from the portfolio average. Three percent of Q4 activity was in the equity portfolio, a slight pickup from the prior four quarters. Importantly, one of the differentiating factors between TSLX and other BDCs is TSLX’s investment in non-sponsored companies (private equity portfolio companies are often called "sponsored companies"). During 2025, approximately 50 percent of TSLX’s investments were in non-sponsored companies. This differentiator reduces the overlap with other BDCs, allowing for diversification.

TSLX maintains high diversification, with most industries representing less than 10 percent of the portfolio (with the exception of three industries). While the portfolio is diversified, the top ten industries represent 87 percent of the total. The largest industry exposure is internet services, at 18.3 percent (which is large considering current concerns). In reality, however, their software exposure is actually much larger than the industry generally (or at least as reported), which is explained below. Importantly, TSLX’s average investment size is 0.9 percent, limiting the impact of any one company on the results.

To be clear, the software exposure of TSLX is portrayed differently than many BDCs. From the earnings call (emphasis mine):

We understand many of our peers map the industry exposure differently from us with a specific software classification, which is intended to illustrate enterprise software exposure. We do not view software as a stand-alone industry, but instead, we view it as a mission-critical tool that enables a broad range of end-user markets.

For that reason, our industry disclosure is organized by end market, such as health care, business services and financial services rather than by specific products or delivery mechanisms used to serve those markets. We believe this is a better approach to risk management as the primary driver of credit performance is the health and demand of the end markets being served rather than the technology used to deliver the service.

At this moment in time, however, we felt it beneficial to our stakeholders to provide a more comparable figure to our peers. We have mapped our portfolio to enterprise software exposure, which comprises approximately 40% of our total portfolio by fair value.

The credit statistics of this portfolio are largely consistent with the overall portfolio, including a weighted average LTV of 40%, LTM top line growth of approximately 9% and LTM earnings growth of approximately 15%.

Forty percent of the portfolio would traditionally map to software. This is a significant exposure, larger than most BDCs report. While disconcerting, TSLX has a long history in the space, and an LTV of 40 percent helps mitigate the downside.

Portfolio Quality

Nearly 90% of TSLX’s lending is in the form of "first lien", or priority debt. As I continually caution, however, this is not always what it seems. Within the first lien, you can also have "First Lien, First Out" and "First Lien, Last Out" dynamics where there is a waterfall payment structure within the first lien bucket. This is often seen within a "unitranche" structure, where the borrower sees one tranche, but there are waterfall agreements between the lenders. TSLX neither reports the amount of unitranche loans within the first lien bucket nor do they specify the amount of FLFO or FLLO (which would be great information), they simply report that they have unitranche and FLLO. Not all first liens are equal.

One of the first things folks notice when looking at the characteristics is the portfolio yield. TSLX’s portfolio yield is high, both in an absolute sense and relative to the peer group (as you will see in the comparison section). By default (no pun intended), this is because they have a different risk profile. I would call TSLX an “opportunistic lender” where they are lending to companies with a couple of warts, but they recognize the value of the business. This is shown through commentary management made in their Q4 call:

On funding trends for the year, nearly half of fundings were off the run in what we consider Lane 2, challenged businesses with good asset bases; and Lane 3, good businesses with challenged capital structures.

In other words, TSLX is focused on capturing the complexity premium and adding alpha through underwriting. This has been their bread and butter and has, thus far, worked out well for investors.

Despite their focus on complexity, or underwriting alpha, non-accruals have remained low, ending 2025 at 0.60 percent, down from the third quarter’s 1.20 percent and year-end 2024’s 1.40 percent. Moreover, approximately 98 percent of the portfolio falls within their top two rating categories.

TSLX also differentiates on origination or vintage date. Valuations were high, and rates were low until 2023, so higher portfolio yields and spreads became available in 2023 (recall market drawdowns and returns that year). Again, as they stated on their Q4 call:

“We have less exposure to pre-2022 vintages than our peers. I think today, we sit at about 20% to 25% of NAV. The vast majority of our portfolio we originated post the rate hiking cycle in 2023 and 2024. We've been less active of late as the markets have gotten tighter. But yes, so about 20% of NAV before 2022, which is much different than our peers.”

When looking at the firm’s internal rating distributions, note that there has been a shift in ratings from grade one to grade two, showing a reduction in overall quality. Also note, however, that grades five and three have also been reduced.

The description of grades one and two from TSLX’s 10-K:

An investment is rated 1 if, in the opinion of the Adviser, it is performing as agreed and there are no concerns about the portfolio company’s performance or ability to meet covenant requirements. For these investments, the Adviser generally prepares monthly reports on investment performance and intensive quarterly asset reviews. An investment is rated 2 if it is performing as agreed, but, in the opinion of the Adviser, there may be concerns about the company’s operating performance or trends in the industry. For these investments, in addition to monthly reports and quarterly asset reviews, the Adviser also researches any areas of concern with the objective of early intervention with the portfolio company.

Company comments on software exposure

Long-time followers will know that our team has been investing in technology-related businesses for more than 2 decades, and we've navigated multiple periods of significant change. In each case, there were predictions of the demise of incumbents or the erosion of margins. With hindsight, those shifts tended to expand addressable markets and create opportunities for those who could distinguish between durable and fragile business models.

So rather than AI bridging a moat that protected businesses and their margins, we see AI as leveling the playing field on development costs that does not fundamentally change the intrinsic moats that protect a business. Existing enterprise software companies should benefit from the shift in the cost curve if they are well managed and have limited technical debt.

They own the distribution, they own the customer relationship, and they possess deep domain expertise. These moats, data integration, network effects and regulatory complexity are incredibly difficult for a new entrant to come in and displace even in a world where it is faster and cheaper to write code.

Less growth means fundamental valuations of these assets is lower, but that doesn't mean they aren't generally creditworthy. If you look at the credit spreads since the beginning of the year of public enterprise software companies and how little they have widened about 10 to 20 basis points on average compared to the compression in the TEV multiples about 2 to 3 turns or about 15% on average, it illustrates this point. For more levered private software companies, we see broadly syndicated loan spreads about 50 to 100 basis points wider versus the beginning of the year. The market is re-rating the equity risk, but the credit remains resilient.

Liquidity

The company utilizes a mix of funding sources, with a strategic emphasis on unsecured borrowings to maintain financial flexibility. For the most part, their debt is well laddered, with no one year presenting an issue (in normal market conditions).

Importantly, their unsecured debt is fully swapped to a floating rate, making their cost of debt capital directionally matched to 96 percent of their investment portfolio. This will help mitigate MIN compression due to interest rates.

Dividend Stability

TSLX maintained their base dividend, which was 113 percent covered by NII. This is one of the highest coverage ratios of the peer group, and, while sequentially lower, management intends to keep the base dividend (adding supplemental dividends when practicable). As TSLX bases the dividend off the forward curve rather than current NII, the dividend should continue to be stable despite an anticipated move in short-term rates. TSLX also has approximately $1.21 in spillover income as of 12/31/25 which can be used to support the dividend should it be required.

As it is widely expected (and telegraphed) that the Federal Reserve will be reducing their policy rate further this year, there will be an impact on portfolio yield and net interest income.

If we look at the expectations for the remainder of the year, we see three cuts, which (roughly) reduce NII by about $10mm.

There are currently 2-3 further policy rate cuts expected this year, which means that the yields on the investments will fall further. Fortunately, as mentioned in the liquidity section, nearly all of their debt is swapped to floating rate, which is why the NII impact is less than some of their peers.

Then, of course, there is PIK income. Recall that PIK income is not paid in cash, but rather as an increase in the principal of the loan. While it is treated as income, it cannot be paid out in a distribution. At nearly six percent of investment income, TSLX is middle of the road of the peer group in terms of the amount of PIK income.

As PIK income cannot be paid out as a distribution, firms generally use their revolver to cover the gap, increasing the cost of funds.

Company comments on the dividend:

We look at the spillover as one option to help with smoothing over time and the prioritization around consistency, which we do think is important. At the end of the day, though, Melissa, earnings are going to drive the dividend power of the business. The Board is also going to remain focused on prioritizing transparency. If you kind of look at net interest income, and Dave commented on this, there's both headwinds and tailwinds as we talked about in prior quarters. From an asset yield point of view, I would highlight the fact that from an NII impact perspective, you see about a $0.015 impact associated with each 25 basis point cut from the Fed.

If you see significant cuts kind of going out in the future, it may not be a perfect offset, but we're going to continue to be laser-focused on optimizing ROE, creating value for investors, and that includes paying a compelling distribution that the core earnings can support over time. [ link to the earnings call]

Bottom line on dividend stability: The base dividend should continue to remain stable due to the coverage ratio, the moderate PIK exposure, the use of the supplemental dividend as a “leveler” depending on NII, and their comfortable spillover.

Comparisons

While there are many BDCs, I tend to group TSLX in with the following peer group:

Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC), Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC), Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL), Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL), and Golub Capital (GBDC).

An overview of the peer group statistics (as of the most recent reported quarter) shows that TSLX is in decent shape on non-accruals, dividend coverage, leverage, and portfolio yield is the highest in the peer group.

Some color on performance.

Fee Structure

As folks seemed to like the fee structure aspect, here is the waterfall for TSLX’s fees.

The 6% hurdle rate is consistent with the majority of the peer group, as are the incentive fees and the capital gains fees.

Hurdle: 1.5% per quarter (6% annual)

The adviser earns zero incentive fees until the shareholder return exceeds 1.5% of the beginning NAV.

Catch-up: The adviser receives 100% of the income from 1.5% to 1.82% (6% to 7.28% annually).

This “catch-up band” ensures that once the hurdle is cleared, the adviser’s share of total income above the hurdle reaches the full 17.5% rate. In other words, the adviser takes all incremental income in this band.

Full Rate: 17.5% of all income above 1.82% (7.28% annual).

After the catch-up is completed, the adviser receives 17.5% of every additional dollar of pre-fee NII.

Importantly, TSLX charges the base management fee on the company's gross assets calculated using the values at the end of the two most recently completed calendar quarters, adjusted for any share issuances or repurchases during the period. As the fee can effectively only be charged to shareholders, the “fee on equity” is higher than the stated fee on assets. When adjusted for leverage, the fee on equity is ~3.2% (1.5% * (1+1.10)), which is comparable to ARCC and OBDC. Obviously, this leverage also helps generate the NII that provides the returns.

There is also a “step down” in place based on leverage. If the debt-to-equity exceeds 1x (an asset coverage greater than 200%), the fee on the assets financed steps down to 1% on the gross assets in excess of 200% asset coverage. In the company’s documents, this is referred to as a fee waiver.

Errata

From the Q4/2025 earnings release and call.

During Q4, affiliates of Sixth Street, including TSLX, formed Structured Credit Partners JV, LLC (“SCP”), a joint venture between TSLX, Sixth Street Lending Partners, and two BDCs managed by Carlyle Group Inc. SCP is focused on investing in broadly syndicated first lien senior secured loans, financed with long-term, non-mark-to-market, and predominantly investment-grade-rated CLO debt managed by affiliates of Sixth Street or Carlyle on a no-fee basis. TSLX’s total commitment to the joint venture is $200 million. As of December 31, 2025, SCP had not commenced operations, and no capital had been contributed to SCP.

Additional Resources

December 31, 2025 earnings call

December 31, 2025 earnings presentation

December 31, 2025 10K.

data.title: WDI May Benefit From Shifting Interest Rate Policy
data.description:
The Western Asset Diversified Income Fund (WDI) is a closed-end fund designed to provide investors with fixed income exposure across a wide variety of issuances. The strategy is presently allocated most heavily in high-yield credit with a proportional allocation towards lower-rated issuances, offering investors an appealing distribution yield of 12.41% while the portfolio trades at a modest -3.04% discount to NAV. Despite the relatively riskier allocation to high-yield credit, WDI offers broad diversification across issuances where no single issuer holds substantial weight in the portfolio.

With the US macroeconomic outlook turning more positive into the start of 2026, I believe WDI can potentially be an appealing fixed income strategy for investors seeking high-yield, managed exposure.

Investment Thesis for WDI

High-yield issuances generally trade in a similar fashion to the broad equity market given the risky nature of these issuances relative to investment-grade issuances. This can present investors with a unique opportunity to buy into a strategy like WDI with the expectation that the market will continue to present positive returns. Granted, there are some intricacies that may result in a certain amount of performance decoupling, such as economic outlook, interest rate policy, and market liquidity, that may impact high-yield performance, though the general rule is that when the equity market is growing, high-yields may be expected to follow suit.

As a component of the corporate bond sleeve, WDI had larger exposures in energy at 11.6%, industrials at 11.9%, consumer discretionary at 13.1%, and communications services at 11.5%.

One of the potential challenges in the portfolio strategy is the shifting tide in consumer discretionary as inflationary costs begin to shift corporate strategy. Accordingly, the Wall Street Journal recently reported that consumer-facing businesses are expected to begin raising prices in 2026 to protect margins as a result of pricing pressures due to rising costs from tariffs and salary increases.

This could potentially influence the perception of value across the consumer market as to whether growth may be sustained and whether the quality of bond issuances will be maintained. If the consumer market were to face challenges in maintaining sales volumes while raising prices, there could potentially be a major disruption to a decent proportion of WDI’s corporate bond portfolio.

Another area of concern is more tied to macroeconomic factors. As of its q3’25 report, WDI had significant exposure to floating-rate issuances as part of its collateralized mortgage obligations [CMOs] at 23.9%, asset-backed securities [ABSs] at 22.7%, and senior loans at 15.5% sleeves. That being said, the portfolio is managed dynamically, and positions & concentrations may have shifted since WDI’s q3’25 report; however, exposure to floating-rate issuances appears to remain prevalent in the portfolio. Floating-rate issuances are priced differently from fixed-rate issuances in that the price of floating-rates is adjusted in accordance with interest rate policy rather than to the inverse, as seen for fixed rates. This may mean that a proportion of the portfolio may experience a certain degree of downward repricing if the Federal Reserve were to continue easing interest rates in 2026.

Wall Street appears to be becoming divided on expected rate policy going forward, with some economists expecting 0-75bps cuts in 2026 for a target rate ranging from 2.75-3.75% and others expecting a rate hike by q4’26 or in 2027. This level of uncertainty in the market may create opportunity for investors in WDI as bonds may become a more appealing speculative trade given the growing division. As it relates to WDI, if rate cuts were to persist throughout 2026, WDI’s floating-rate issuance holdings may be repriced lower on the basis of lower coupon payments. If rates are held steady or hiked, WDI could become that much more appealing as the relative price of these floating-rate issuances may appreciate. The contrasting factor is that the fixed-rate corporate bonds in the portfolio will theoretically do the opposite in terms of rate policy and pricing, potentially creating some neutrality in the portfolio’s assets.

Looking at the US macroeconomic data, the economy appears to be strengthening, with US manufacturing output improving and the rate of inflation and unemployment coming down. Overall, I believe it may be too early to determine whether this is a trend or short-term adjustments given the broader scope of the theme.

Accordingly, indicators like customers’ inventories as presented in the ISM-PMI Manufacturing Index either suggest a need to replenish inventory levels or a transition to just-in-time production. I believe many of the indicators presented in the PMI report may be associated with inventories, driving up new orders, backlog, and deliveries. If these levels are maintained going forward, I believe this may be a signal that the US economy is strengthening and may deem no further rate cuts for 2026. Though I’m not taking a rate hike off the table, I believe the Fed will be focused on potential ripple effects from tariff costs and whether they do ultimately bring back an increasing inflation rate.

About Western Asset Diversified Income Fund

WDI was launched on June 24, 2021 under Franklin Templeton as a closed-end fund designed to provide investors with monthly income. WDI has a 161bps management fee for a gross expense ratio of 466bps inclusive of interest expenses and operating expenses; WDI has $769mm in net assets.

WDI pays out an appealing monthly distribution rate with a forward rate of $1.78/share, yielding 12.41%.

WDI has performed exceptionally well amongst its peers when comparing 3-year historical total returns, though it underperformed at the 1-year mark.

Risks Related to WDI

WDI may expose investors to certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. High-yield issuances may present investors with greater credit risk and potential market volatility given how these issuances are priced to the market. WDI may exhibit interest rate risk, particularly due to its floating-rate exposure; if the Federal Reserve were to continue its rate-easing cycle, a large component of the portfolio could face some pricing pressures.

Final Thoughts

WDI may be an appealing fixed income strategy for investors seeking diversified high-yield exposure. WDI takes a rotational approach to investing in the debt market, providing investors with a dynamic portfolio strategy that aims to outperform. Given the improving macroeconomic outlook in the US, I believe WDI could perform well going forward; I am recommending this CEF with a Buy rating.

data.title: CNH Industrial: Cycle Ag Recovery Leverage, But Not A Lot Else Yet
data.description:
When a company is saying a lot of the same things for five years but evidence of progress is limited, I think it’s fair to be skeptical of where the company is heading. Such is the case for me with CNH Industrial (CNH). There have been seven CEOs since 2013 if you count interim positions, and throughout that time I feel like the narrative on streamlining the business, improving operational efficiency, and investing in tech hasn’t changed much, but the results haven’t really come through.

Since my last update four years ago, CNH shares are down about 12%, significantly underperforming ag rivals like AGCO (AGCO), Deere (DE), and Kubota (KUBTY), as well as construction comps like Caterpillar (CAT) and Komatsu (KMTUY).

I see a lot of the same issues and challenges today, including a “fast follower” tech philosophy in agriculture that doesn’t seem to really be working out, and sub-scale construction operations. The current CEO has been on the job since 2024 and deserves more time before judging these latest self-help efforts, but at this point I don’t really like CNH beyond its leverage to a broader recovery in agriculture capex in 2027.

A Beat, With Business Pulled Forward

CNH did beat sell-side expectations for the fourth quarter, but that upside was largely driven by some business that was pulled forward into the fourth quarter from 2026. Absent that benefit, results would have been more or less as expected.

Revenue rose 3% in constant currency, beating by 5%, with industrial revenue up 5% and beating by 4%. Agricultural equipment sales were up 3%, beating by 4%, while Construction rose 17%, beating by 6%. Financial Services revenue declined 6%, beating by 2%.

Industrial gross margin declined 110bp to 19%, with Ag down 60bp to 20% and Construction down 440bp to 11.4%. Adjusted operating income declined 45%, with Industrial up 21% and Financial Services down 7%. Within Industrial, Ag earnings declined 5% (margin down 70bp to 6.5%), with a 140bp margin beat, while Construction fell 72% with margin down 190bp to 0.6% (beating by 90bp).

Looking at ag comps, AGCO reported a 3% year-over-year comparable decline in its business, with high-single-digit declines in North America and South America. Deere reported 3% sales growth in its Production and Precision Ag business (as well as 24% growth in Small Ag & Turf, where overlap/comparability is limited).

While I don’t believe that Deere quantified organic growth for Production & Precision Ag, the release did say that “sales increased for the quarter as a result of the positive effects of foreign currency translation.” Even with the pulled-forward business, then, CNH did modestly outperform in the ag business this quarter.

Weak Guidance As The Cycle Bottoms Out

While many heavy equipment stocks, agriculture included, are trading on the expectation of bottoming/recovering markets, CNH’s guidance suggests that the bottom isn’t in yet.

Management said that they’re expecting weaker demand in 2026 ahead of a recovery in 2027, and guided to a mid-single-digit decline in Ag segment revenue in 2026 (on a constant currency basis). At the midpoint, that’s about 3% below Street expectations. The guide for Construction was incrementally weaker, with the business expected to be down slightly in constant currency, and about 5% below the Street at the midpoint.

Segment earnings are likewise expected to be meaningfully weaker than expected. Overall industrial EBIT was guided about 45% lower than expected (about $360M lower), with Ag about $200M weaker (or about 25% below prior expectations). Bottom-line EPS was guided to $0.35 to $0.45 versus $0.54, or a 26% downward revision at the midpoint.

Street optimism over improving conditions notwithstanding, there are good reasons to expect another year of weak ag capex demand. Low crop prices, higher input costs, and higher interest rates have squeezed farmers, and higher machinery costs (with price hikes tied to cost inflation, tariffs, and other factors) have led many to focus on extending the life of their existing equipment.

On a more positive note, though, in-service fleet ages are above long-term averages, and efforts to extend the life of machinery can only go so far. Moreover, newer tech-enabled equipment from Deere, CNH, et al. offers meaningful operational savings opportunities through higher yields and more efficient input usage (optimized planting, spraying, et al.).

Farmer incomes should also improve somewhat; bailouts from the U.S. government will likely go more toward debt repayment, and aren’t enough to drive capex spending, but shifts from corn to soy should drive incrementally higher incomes. Assuming no major additional disruptions to trade relations, 2027 should be a strong recovery year for equipment spending.

More Self-Help Is Needed

There is not much that CNH can do to influence the broader macroeconomic backdrop that drives agricultural capex, but there’s still a lot of self-help potential within the business.

CNH has talked for some time about improving core margins in the Ag business, but progress has been inconsistent at best. While weak end-market conditions, input cost inflation, and tariffs are all notable complicating factors, the Street’s patience for extended margin improvement initiatives only lasts so long.

Management is targeting 16%-17% Ag margins in 2030 versus 10.5% in 2024 and 6.2% in 2025, as well as 7% to 8% margins in Construction (versus 2.3% in ’25 and 5.5% in ’24). While the basic “hows” all sound reasonable—strategic sourcing, manufacturing/plant efficiencies, more tech-enabled sales, and so on—that's been the plan for some time now, and the results haven’t come through yet.

Likewise with the company’s investments in tech and automation. With the acquisition of Raven years ago (and some additional M&A since then), CNH has been able to in-house about 80% of its capabilities (from 25% pre-Raven), but the company still lags Deere when it comes to leading-edge machinery automation and precision agriculture. At around 5% of sales, precision ag is still a modest contributor to the business, and I think this will be a tough gap to close.

On a more positive note, I do like CNH’s tech positioning in soil preparation, including optimized tilling and planting. I also like the company’s efforts in specialty crops. Only about 10% of fruit-picking is currently mechanized, and CNH has strong leadership in areas like grapes and olives. I believe a lot of these core competencies are transferable to other product types, and this is an area where I’d really like to see the company invest. I think there’s a real chance of differentiation here, particularly as sourcing labor is likely to become more challenging in many markets in the years to come.

I also think management should fundamentally rethink its position with Construction, and this was something I thought back at the time of that last article as well. The company is basically an also-ran in markets like excavators and wheel-loaders, and marginally better-positioned in skid-steer loaders.

This lack of scale is an ongoing drag for the business, and I don’t see CNH offering enough in terms of automation or specialty features to offset it. I do still believe the company could do better by really focusing on niche opportunities like small/compact products and products designed for niche applications (not unlike specialty harvesting in ag), but management hasn’t gone that way, and I see this business as an ongoing drag.

The Outlook

I do expect CNH’s business to bottom in 2026 and I expect the recovery cycle to drive high single-digit annualized revenue growth, with at least a year or two of double-digit growth. Long term, though, I expect that growth to slow to something more in the 3% to 4% range.

I expect the recovery cycle to drive segment margins back into the low double-digits, but I have my doubts that management will be able to attain and maintain their mid-teens target for the Ag segment in 2030 and beyond. I do expect some degree of overall operational improvement, though, and that should drive free cash flow margins into the 5%-6% range (likely high single-digits to low double-digits in the best years), which is better than the long-term average and enough to drive high single-digit long-term adjusted FCF growth.

Discounted cash flow suggests a high single-digit annualized total return potential. A modified EBITDA approach (using margins/returns, and then factoring in the value of the financial services business) suggests a 10.5x forward multiple; using that multiple on FY’28 EBITDA (when the recovery should be well in place) and then discounting back two years gets me to a fair value of about $15/share today.

The Bottom Line

CNH does look undervalued, even with the shares having already run on growing investor optimism that heavy machinery markets are bottoming and/or starting to recover. I do see CNH as participating in end-market recoveries, and I am more bullish on construction equipment in 2026 and agriculture in 2027.

There’s definitely upside here if management can execute on its operational improvement targets, and particularly if it can close some of the gap with Deere in terms of tech capabilities and perception. I haven’t seen enough progress to like this name as a core holding, but as a directional trade, I can still see worthwhile upside for investors who want that sort of investment idea.
